items.rationale: Believes decentralization is the key unlock that democratizes the internet, obviates many regulatory risks, and lets network effects accrue to users rather than incumbents.

citations.quote: We're entering a new era of decentralization.
The country that properly incentivizes decentralization is going to win web3 and reap the benefits.
More Decentralization = More Innovation

items.rationale: Personally helped create the Decentralized Unincorporated Nonprofit Association; sees it as a breakthrough giving DAOs legal existence, tax ability, limited liability and privacy without sacrificing decentralization.
items.age_note: Most recent and most intense theme (March 2024); supersedes his earlier preference for the California/Texas UNA forms.

citations.quote: Wyoming just enacted a game-changing law for web3... the Decentralized Unincorporated Nonprofit Association (DUNA)
This brings much needed certainty to web3 builders, enabling them to get back to doing what they do best: BUILD.

items.rationale: His signature policy framework: regulate for-profit businesses/apps, not the underlying software protocols, analogous to regulating Gmail not SMTP. Foundation for all his advocacy.

citations.quote: Regulate apps, not protocols. Regulate businesses, not software.
we have a complete foundation for a regulation-agnostic web3 framework.

items.rationale: Excited tokens have a far bigger design space than equity; favors application tokens that coordinate developers/B2B and ensure credible neutrality over user-acquisition B2C speculation.

citations.quote: Tokens aren't equity. The design space is so much bigger.
Application token models should focus more on B2B and less on B2C.

items.rationale: Sees app-layer diversity and distributing tokens among clients/apps as critical for governance minimization, credible neutrality, and richer services like decentralized social.

citations.quote: how clients drive decentralization and how tokens can align incentives among clients and ensure credible neutrality of the protocol.
smart contract protocols should be distributing tokens among the clients/apps that build on top of them.

items.rationale: Excited about new governance best practices that hold leadership accountable and minimize governance, solving low participation and capture problems that prior DAO models failed to fix.

citations.quote: 'Machiavellian DAOs' can foster true decentralization in web3.
governance minimization is the most important design guideline... easier to minimize governance if you have a robust app/client layer

items.rationale: Views legitimate decentralized stablecoins as strategically important counterweight to surveillance-state CBDCs and winner-take-all centralized issuers.

citations.quote: this should further impress upon people the importance of legitimate decentralized stablecoin projects.
Concentrated power in the hands of any single stablecoin issuer undermines decentralization across web3.

items.rationale: Strong civil-liberties framing; sees privacy tech (and token extensions like confidential transfers) as a core web3 value and constitutional battleground — though acknowledges illicit-finance tension.

citations.quote: the right to be let alone is indeed the beginning of all freedom.
Very excited to get to work on the privacy frontier again

items.rationale: Optimistic the UK's 'same risk, same regulatory outcome' approach, exclusion of decentralized stablecoins, and mandate for regulators to foster innovation could make it a web3 hub.
items.age_note: Stated mid-2023 (May/June) and lightly reaffirmed Oct 2023; not restated in 2024.

citations.quote: the UK empowering its financial regulators to foster innovation is brilliant.
there is cause for significant optimism, but the next 18 months are critical.

items.rationale: Excited about collaborative security organizations producing novel solutions that elevate security across the sector.

citations.quote: There are going to be a lot of truly novel solutions coming out of this org, which should elevate security across the sector.

items.rationale: Champions players truly owning interoperable in-game assets and the metaverse accruing to consumers not corporations; an early-interest sector for him.
items.age_note: Enthusiasm dates to 2022-10; by 2023-10 his framing shifted to defending gaming from California AB 39 rather than pure excitement.

citations.quote: it paves the way for corporations, not consumers, to own the metaverse.
Highly recommend this for anyone working in web3 gaming.

items.rationale: His single largest concern: believes the SEC's endgame is to ban the technology via enforcement, fostering uncertainty that helps bad actors and drives builders offshore.
items.age_note: Consistent and intensifying from 2022 through 2024; tied to current administration.

citations.quote: the SEC's endgame for crypto/web3 in the United States is about one thing: banning the technology.
regulation-by-enforcement is no substitute for formal rulemaking and public comment processes

items.rationale: Four-plus years without SEC guidance and lack of clear rules incentivize corner-cutting and 'decentralization theater', and are the biggest brake on US development and oversight.

citations.quote: regulatory uncertainty is already the biggest economic drag on the sector.
when the rules aren't clear and evenly enforced, builders are incentivized to cut corners... decentralization theater.

items.rationale: Fears legislation treating crypto as whatever maximizes surveillance, ignoring the Third-Party Doctrine and the peer-to-peer privacy expectation; frames it as civil-liberties erosion.

citations.quote: Elizabeth Warren's Patriot Act Part 2.
their answer to everything is 'more surveillance'... don't respect Americans' right to privacy.

items.rationale: His clearest market-structure concern: fixed/non-dilutable token allocations transfer value from founders to investors and misalign incentives; short lockups drive pump-and-dumps and VC dumping on retail.

citations.quote: Fixed token allocations are predatory.
If a VC pushes to shorten your token lockup... 'we intend to dump our bags as soon as you'll let us'.

items.rationale: Concerned the broker-reporting regime and >$10k reporting rules are unworkable overreach that threaten privacy and impose impossible obligations.

citations.quote: The industry's collective effort to push back against Treasury's expansive broker reporting regime has been remarkable.
the IRS commissioner from 1 year ago writes this about the IRS's rule expansion for broker reporting?

items.rationale: Worries one-size-fits-all licensing sweeps in video games, punishes emergency-pause functions, threatens decentralization and stablecoin innovation, and could devastate indie game developers.

citations.quote: Applying a one-size-fits-all reg regime to financial institutions and video games is an obvious mistake.
regulations that could consolidate power among centralized actors in crypto (like California's new AB 39).

items.rationale: Calls out funds requiring companies to pay 2% of token supply for low-effort, ChatGPT-grade 'launch services' on top of their investment — predatory and a sign of intent to dump.

citations.quote: If a VC requires you to pay them tokens for their help launching your token... They're going to dump your token

items.rationale: Concerned that demonstrations of programmable transaction controls inch toward a surveillance-state CBDC; treats it as reason to back decentralized stablecoins.

citations.quote: Agree this inches closer to a potential surveillance state CBDC.

items.rationale: Post-Tornado Cash, worries OFAC could sanction a token's smart contract mid-process, making an entire token supply toxic and harming legitimate users.

citations.quote: we have to consider smart contract sanctioning a viable attack vector.

items.rationale: Suspects engineered compliant-pathway theater designed to give SEC control over which assets list and dissuade Congress from passing market-structure legislation.
items.age_note: Heavily analyzed mid-2023; not restated in 2024.

citations.quote: the listing of a digital asset on Prometheum being anything other than a death sentence for most projects?

items.rationale: Acknowledges high FDV/low float is a significant problem, exacerbated by short chunky lockups; favors gradual lockups tied to decentralization milestones.

citations.quote: Agree that high FDV/low float is a significant problem, but longer/gradual lockups are a better approach

items.rationale: Confident ETH is a sufficiently decentralized commodity, not a security; argues the SEC effectively conceded this by approving ETH futures, strengthening the case for a spot ETH ETF.
items.age_note: This is a legal/regulatory confidence view, not a price call.

citations.quote: The SEC capitulated on the question of whether ETH is a security in October when it approved the ETH futures products.
they've implicitly consented to ETH being a commodity and not a security.

items.rationale: Views USDC as likely legally sound — Circle KYCs depositors but isn't the issuer of a yield product and doesn't rehypothecate, distinguishing it from UST/Anchor.
items.age_note: A legal-risk assessment, not an investment endorsement.

citations.quote: USDC is likely okay, but yield bearing stablecoins should take note.

items.rationale: Repeatedly cites Liquity as the 'star example' of a properly decentralized, immutable DeFi structure and design ideal.
items.age_note: Praised as a structural model, not as a trade.

citations.quote: I've long used @LiquityProtocol as the star example from DeFi of this type of structure.

items.rationale: Uses Terra as the cautionary archetype: an undercollateralized, non-decentralized stablecoin packaged with Anchor's 'bullshit 20% yield' as an unregistered securities offering — and warns CeFi yield-on-stablecoin products to take note.
items.age_note: Retrospective/cautionary; Terra had already collapsed, but he invokes it as a forward warning for yield-bearing stablecoins.

citations.quote: Terra was both the issuer/promoter of UST and the operator of Anchor... its bullshit 20% yield, which also wasn't decentralized.
CeFi products offering yield on stablecoins to US retail should be careful

risk_regime.rationale: Structurally bullish on web3/decentralization as the future of the internet — he left a BigLaw partnership to go all-in and repeatedly tells founders to build and 'Stay The Course.' But his optimism is explicitly conditioned on regulatory survival: he sees acute hostility from the current SEC/Warren faction as the dominant near-term risk, so his posture is long-horizon builder-bullish, not a short-term market call. He is essentially silent on price/macro and rarely expresses token-trading views.

notes: Miles Jennings is a16z crypto's General Counsel/head of decentralization, so his corpus is ~90% legal, policy, and token/DAO-structure commentary rather than investment theses; 'sectors' here are largely regulatory and structural themes, and genuine token-price views are thin (ETH/USDC/Liquity entries are legal-risk or design assessments, not buy calls). Most recent statements (Jan–Mar 2024) center on the Wyoming DUNA, SEC v. Coinbase, predatory VC token terms, and privacy/surveillance — these were weighted most heavily. Notable consistency rather than reversal across 18 months: his 'regulate apps not protocols,' decentralization-matters-for-Howey, and anti-Gensler stances are stable and reinforced over time. One evolution worth flagging: he openly conceded to Adam Cochran on 2024-03-20 ('I disagreed with you then and I was wrong'), and his tone toward the SEC hardened from 'constructive partner' framing (2022-2023) to alleging deliberate, politicized bad faith by 2024. Web3 gaming and UK-optimism are older/persisted themes not restated in 2024. He expresses essentially no view on crypto market cycles, BTC, or macro positioning.
